answer:
**Only one poem in *Bang-e-Dara* explicitly addresses the Prophet Muhammad (as the direct interlocutor in a visionary dialogue):** "Huzoor Risalat Ma'ab Mein" (*In the Exalted Prophet's Presence*), Chapter 11 of Part 3.[^1] 

In this naat (ode of praise), Iqbal imagines being led by angels to the Prophet's court, where the Prophet directly addresses him as "O Nightingale of Hijaz's garden-bower," praises his devotional poetry, and asks what gift he brings from the worldly garden. Iqbal offers a "phial of crystal bright" filled with the blood of Tripoli's martyrs, symbolizing the ummah's enduring honor through sacrifice.[^2] 

Numerous other poems reference the Prophet reverentially—e.g., invoking his name for revival (*Dil se jo baat nikalti hai...*, Ch. 4, Part 3),[^3] his ummah's legacy (*Shikwa*, Ch. 20, Part 3),[^4] followers as "Mustafavi" (*Wataniyat*, Ch. 25, Part 3),[^5] or his resting place (*Qita*, Ch. 16, Part 3)[^6]—but none feature direct address or dialogue with him.[^7] Parts 1 and 2 lack even these explicit references in their English translations.[^8]

With only one such poem, there is no cluster to assess for doctrinal coherence. It stands alone as a singular, mystical encounter emphasizing poetic devotion, ummah solidarity, and sacrificial fidelity to the Prophet's light—untied to a specific historical occasion, unlike Iqbal's occasional verses elsewhere (e.g., *Shikwa* as a communal lament).

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 11 (*Huzoor Risalat Ma'ab Mein*), [translation/](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/11-huzoor-risalat-maab-mein/translation/).

[^2]: Ibid., [p-c8ea02](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/11-huzoor-risalat-maab-mein/translation/#p-c8ea02).

[^3]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 4 (*Dil se jo baat nikalti hai asar rakhti hai*), [translation/](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/#p-9aa6ea).

[^4]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 20 (*Shikwa*), [translation/](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/20-shikwa/translation/).

[^5]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 25 (*Wataniyat*), [translation/](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-2737d5).

[^6]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 16 (*Qita*), [translation/](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/16-qita/translation/#p-82af40).

[^7]: No other chapters in Parts 1–3 contain direct apostrophe (e.g., "O Prophet") per corpus search results for "Prophet," "Muhammad," "Mustafa," and "Messenger."

[^8]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1* and *Part 2* chapter wikis and searches yield no hits; e.g., Part 1 Ch. 26 mentions biblical prophets generically, [p-a9f72d](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/26-hindustani-bachon-ka-qaumi-geet/translation/#p-a9f72d).
